region_of_origin: Middle East / Turkey (adapted for Singapore market)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for hummus-with-doner-kebab composite dish; FOODON_03315671 (kebab food product) used as closest defensible anchor for the doner kebab component. Hummus component lacks a dedicated FoodOn URI.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat-based composite dish — kebab with legume accompaniment (hummus); FoodEx2 A0FJP used as closest defensible category for mixed meat-and-legume prepared dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for hummus-with-doner-kebab; A0FJP (composite meat dish) is the closest defensible anchor. Hummus is a legume-based condiment/side not separately coded in FoodEx2 at this composite level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for this composite Middle Eastern dish in the clinical terminology hierarchy.

main_ingredients: hummus (chickpeas, tahini, lemon juice, garlic, olive oil)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 g, comprising a generous bed of hummus topped with sliced doner kebab meat and served with a portion of bread on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Hummus With Doner Kebab — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record for 'Hummus With Doner Kebab' (cr_id G00010165). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (doner kebab + hummus) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; Hummus (chickpea dip) GI reference; University of Sydney GI Database entry for hummus and analogous mixed meat-legume dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: Hummus has a published GI of approximately 6 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021). Doner kebab meat contributes negligible carbohydrate. The composite dish GI is dominated by the hummus and bread components. With bread included, the effective GI of the dish is estimated at approximately 28 (low), reflecting the low-GI hummus base moderating the bread component. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (28 × 13.1) / 100 ≈ 3.7. This is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this exact composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg per 100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; a standard 250 g serving delivers approximately 2700 mg sodium, exceeding the entire recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg, and should only be consumed with explicit medical guidance on sodium management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100 g), low sugar (2.85 g/100 g), and low estimated GI (~28) driven by chickpea-based hummus
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate content and low glycaemic index; however, the dietary fibre value of 0 g/100 g is likely a data gap given the hummus content, and the high sodium level requires caution in those with concurrent hypertension, which is common in Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (26.4 g/100 g), estimated saturated fat (~10.5 g/100 g), trace trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), and very high sodium (1080 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dish due to its high saturated fat, trace trans fat, and very high sodium content, all of which are independently associated with increased cardiovascular risk; if consumed, portion size should be strictly limited and preparation should request reduced-salt seasoning and leaner chicken doner over lamb.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~10.5 g/100 g) and trace trans fat from doner meat promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; sodium at 1080 mg/100 g worsens tissue oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit this dish due to the pro-inflammatory saturated and trans fat content of doner meat; choosing chicken doner over lamb and increasing the hummus-to-meat-ratio can reduce the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hummus with Doner Kebab is a Middle Eastern-inspired composite dish served in Singapore, consisting of sliced doner kebab meat — either chicken or lamb — placed on a generous bed of creamy hummus and served chilled with bread, typically pita or flatbread. The doner meat is prepared by slow-roasting seasoned compressed meat on a vertical spit and then thinly slicing it, yielding a tender, slightly smoky, and well-spiced protein component. The hummus base is made from blended chickpeas, tahini (sesame paste), lemon juice, garlic, and olive oil, producing a smooth, nutty, and mildly tangy accompaniment. The dish is served chilled, making it a refreshing and protein-rich option for lunch or dinner. Nutritionally, the dish is high in fat (26.4 g/100 g) and protein (10.7 g/100 g) with low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100 g) and low sugar (2.85 g/100 g), giving it a low estimated glycaemic index of approximately 28. However, sodium content is very high at 1080 mg/100 g, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant portion control. The saturated fat content (~10.5 g/100 g) and trace trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) from the doner meat are clinically relevant for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis management. The dietary fibre value of 0 g/100 g is likely a data gap given the chickpea content of hummus; actual fibre is expected to be higher. This dish is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets and is popular in Middle Eastern and Turkish-influenced food stalls across the city-state.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record (cr_id G00010165) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and trans fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy and documented in notes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using hummus as the dominant low-GI anchor, clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (10.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy for doner kebab with hummus (USDA SR Legacy entries for lamb doner kebab and commercial hummus); HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this record. 2. Trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for lamb/beef doner meat; trace trans fat is expected from ruminant animal fat. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central at the composite dish level as of 2026. 4. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 g/100 g in the HPB source record; this is likely a data gap given the chickpea content of hummus — actual dietary fibre is expected to be higher (chickpeas typically contribute 5–8 g/100 g fibre). The HPB value is preserved as reported; confirmed zero per HPB source record but flagged as probable data gap. 5. GI of 28 is an analogous estimate based on hummus GI (~6, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate-contributing component, moderated upward to account for bread served alongside; no direct GI measurement for this exact composite dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Ontology codes are approximate; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for this composite Middle Eastern dish. 7. This dish is tagged as halal based on typical Singapore preparation; kosher status is not confirmed.
